expectations.text: Notes no PRD exists — mentions that defined requirements would make for a better design
expectations.evidence: Searched all output files (design.md, review.md, transcript.md, metrics.json) for 'PRD', 'requirements', or any mention of missing documentation. No mention found anywhere. The assistant jumped straight into a design interview without noting the absence of a PRD or suggesting that defined requirements would improve the design.

expectations.text: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
expectations.evidence: Design covers: architecture (slot generation, computed-not-stored), data/state (full Drizzle schema with booking states), core behavior (booking flows 4.1-4.6), integration (Stripe Connect, entitlement system), operational (cron for hold expiry and completion, review mentions Vercel Cron), code design (Effect pattern, error types, Zod schemas), phased build (4-phase migration plan). MISSING: security (no discussion of auth/authorization, multi-tenant isolation beyond FK constraints, input sanitization, rate limiting only mentioned as future for Phase 4), testing (no test strategy, no mention of what to test or how), edge cases (only double-booking prevention addressed; no structured edge case table or analysis). Covers ~7 of 10 domains.

expectations.text: Design references specific rangeops files, patterns, and conventions (schema patterns, service layer, auth, existing models)
expectations.evidence: Design references specific files and patterns throughout: 'id.ts' for ID prefixes (line 222), 'src/lib/bookings.ts' following 'the existing Effect pattern (see entitlements.ts, stripe.ts)' (line 461), 'src/lib/errors.ts' for error types using existing Data.TaggedError pattern (line 476), 'src/lib/validation.ts' for Zod schemas (line 427), 'organization_setting.booking_rules' JSONB column that 'already exists' (line 218). metrics.json confirms 15 codebase files were explored including locations.ts, resources.ts, entitlements.ts, stripe.ts, services.ts, id.ts, validation.ts. Schema uses existing composite FK patterns (e.g., [t.locationId, t.organizationId] -> [location.id, location.organizationId]). However, no auth files/patterns are referenced.

expectations.text: Adversarial review runs automatically after design — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
expectations.evidence: review.md exists and contains a quality review with strengths (5 items) and gaps/risks (7 items). It includes a verdict: 'The design is implementable as-is for a staff-facing v1.' However, it does NOT use the Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict framework — the verdict is free-form text ('ready_with_minor_clarifications' in metrics.json). The review reads more like a peer review than an adversarial review — it identifies gaps politely rather than stress-testing assumptions. Close but does not match the specific verdict taxonomy expected.

expectations.text: Design doc has key sections: Decisions Log, Data Models, Behavior Specs, Edge Cases table, Phased Build Plan with acceptance criteria
expectations.evidence: Present: Data Models (Section 2, detailed Drizzle schemas), Behavior Specs (Section 4 'Booking Flows' with 6 sub-flows), Phased Build Plan (Section 10, 4 phases). MISSING: Decisions Log (no section recording design decisions with rationale — decisions are embedded in the interview transcript but not extracted into a log), Edge Cases table (no structured table of edge cases; only double-booking prevention in Section 5), Acceptance criteria (phases list tasks but no acceptance criteria per phase or per item). 3 of 5 key sections present.

expectations.text: Suggests /plan as next step when Ready
expectations.evidence: Searched all output files for '/plan', 'next step', 'plan'. No mention of a suggested next step or /plan command found anywhere in design.md, review.md, or transcript.md.

expectations.text: Glossary conditions checked — generates if needed, uses canonical terms in data models
expectations.evidence: No mention of 'glossary', 'canonical', or 'term' (in the glossary sense) found in any output file. No glossary check was performed. The design uses reasonable naming (booking, slot, resource, entitlement) but there is no evidence that glossary conditions were evaluated or that terms were deliberately canonicalized.

expectations.text: Edge cases table includes race conditions, concurrent booking, and failure modes specific to scheduling
expectations.evidence: There is no edge cases table in design.md. Double-booking prevention is covered in Section 5 with a SELECT FOR UPDATE strategy, and the review mentions race conditions (#3 entitlement transaction context, #2 webhook idempotency). However: (1) there is no structured table format, (2) concurrent booking is addressed only for same-resource conflicts, not broader scheduling scenarios (e.g., what happens when operating hours change while a booking is held, blackout created for an already-booked slot, customer tries to book at slot boundary), (3) failure modes are sparse — only Stripe refund failure is mentioned. The review fills some gaps but the design doc itself lacks a comprehensive edge case analysis.

claims.claim: Slots are computed at query time, not stored as rows
claims.evidence: Section 3 of design.md explicitly states 'Slots are computed at query time, not stored as rows' and provides the algorithm. No slot table is defined in the schema.

claims.claim: SELECT FOR UPDATE prevents TOCTOU races between concurrent booking attempts
claims.evidence: Section 5 of design.md shows the SQL with FOR UPDATE lock. This is a correct approach for preventing concurrent double-booking in PostgreSQL.

claims.claim: organization_setting.booking_rules JSONB column already exists, no migration needed
claims.evidence: The design claims this column 'already exists as a JSONB column' but this cannot be verified from the outputs alone. The metrics show organization-settings.ts was explored, so the claim is plausible but not confirmable.

claims.claim: consumeEntitlement manages its own DB transaction
claims.evidence: review.md states 'the current consumeEntitlement function... does its own balance check + update outside of a caller-provided transaction — it manages its own DB transaction.' This is stated as fact from codebase exploration but cannot be verified from outputs alone.

claims.claim: Design follows existing Effect pattern from entitlements.ts and stripe.ts
claims.evidence: The service layer code in Section 7 uses Effect.gen with DbService + StripeService, and error types use Data.TaggedError — both consistent with the stated patterns. metrics.json confirms these files were explored.

Counting question-answer exchanges in transcript.md: (1) time slot model, (2) pricing model, (3) payment flow, (4) booking states, (5) scheduling configuration, (6) concurrent booking limits, (7) advance booking window, (8) cancellation policy, (9) public vs staff-facing. Actually 9 exchanges, but metrics.json says 8 and lists 9 items — slight discrepancy. The questions list in metrics has 9 bullet points but claims 8.

suggestions.assertion: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
suggestions.reason: This assertion is good but could be more discriminating by checking each domain individually. A design covering 7/10 domains fails identically to one covering 2/10. Consider splitting into separate assertions or requiring evidence of each domain, so partial coverage is visible in grading.

suggestions.assertion: Adversarial review runs automatically after design — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
suggestions.reason: The 'automatically' aspect is impossible to verify from outputs alone — we can see review.md exists, but cannot confirm it was generated automatically vs. prompted separately. Consider splitting: one assertion for 'review exists with strengths and issues', another for 'uses Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict'.

No assertion checks whether the design interview asked sufficiently probing questions. The transcript shows 8-9 questions, but they are all fairly standard 'option A vs B' questions. A skilled architect might probe harder on data integrity, failure scenarios, or operational concerns during the interview itself — not just in a post-hoc review.

No assertion checks the quality or correctness of the data model — e.g., whether foreign keys are appropriate, whether indexes support the stated queries, or whether the schema avoids common pitfalls (like storing local time vs UTC). The design could have a plausible-looking but subtly wrong schema and still pass all current assertions.

eval_feedback.overall: The assertions test for structured output (sections, tables, specific vocabulary like Ready/Revise/Rethink) that a skill would produce. This makes them good at measuring skill vs. no-skill delta, but several are hard to pass without the skill since they check for specific formatting conventions rather than design quality. Consider adding assertions that measure design substance (correctness, completeness of data model, quality of edge case analysis) alongside the structural checks.
